notes.anchor: to make a fund for agrarian distribution
notes.note: Caesar's agrarian law of 59 BC needed land or money to settle Pompey's veterans and the urban poor; Cicero puzzles over where the resources could be found without provoking resistance. The answer, as the next letter reveals, was Caesar's distribution of the public land of Campania.

notes.anchor: Surely the hope is in Publius
notes.note: Heavily ironic. P. Clodius, who became tribune of the plebs in 58, would drive Cicero into exile; here Cicero pretends to long for the "spectacle" of his tribunate as entertainment to be watched with Atticus.

Controversia Mulviana: a dispute, apparently over public land near the Mulvian Bridge north of Rome, in which Atticus was active. Cicero teases that Atticus is really defending the wider interests of all who occupy public land against the tax-farmers.
